ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for garlic noodle; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific garlic noodle entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for garlic noodle or sufficiently specific noodle dish category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of garlic noodle as served in a Singapore café, approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried noodles (mee goreng) / stir-fried noodle dish — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient reference; adapted with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Noodles, egg, cooked, USDA #20112) for macro cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Garlic noodle is a stir-fried wheat noodle dish seasoned with garlic, oil, and soy/oyster sauce. HPB Foodo data for stir-fried noodle dishes (mee goreng / fried noodle category) was used as the primary regional reference. Values were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy cooked egg noodle (USDA #20112) and adjusted upward for oil and seasoning contribution typical of garlic noodle preparation. Estimated per-100 g values reflect a cooked, plated dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for garlic noodle as a prepared dish. GI of 52 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate component. Stir-frying with oil and garlic does not substantially raise GI; fat and protein content may slightly lower the effective GI. GL per serving = (52 × 24.5 × 300 / 100) / 100 = 38.3 g. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as a full serving provides approximately 55% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~38 per serving) from refined wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume garlic noodle in controlled portions; the low GI (52) is favourable but the high carbohydrate load per full serving (~73.5 g carbohydrate per 300 g plate) requires careful carbohydrate counting and pairing with protein or vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with relatively low saturated fat; high sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the relatively low saturated fat content (~0.9 g per 100 g) is favourable, but the high sodium per serving (~1260 mg) warrants caution; garlic itself has evidence-based cardioprotective properties, though the quantity used in a single serving is unlikely to provide therapeutic benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Noodle (蒜香炒面; Mi Bawang Putih) is a popular stir-fried noodle dish served in Singapore cafés and casual dining establishments. It consists of cooked wheat noodles tossed in a fragrant garlic-infused oil, typically seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and garnished with spring onion. The dish is known for its bold, savoury garlic aroma, slightly chewy noodle texture, and glossy oil coating. It is sometimes served as a standalone dish or as a side accompaniment to grilled or fried proteins. The dish is broadly analogous to the Italian aglio e olio pasta preparation but uses Asian wheat noodles and soy-based seasonings. Nutritionally, garlic noodle is a moderate-energy dish (~165 kcal per 100 g) with a carbohydrate-dominant macronutrient profile (~24.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g), moderate fat from cooking oil (~5.5 g per 100 g), and modest protein (~4.8 g per 100 g). Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy and oyster sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category), based on the wheat noodle base, making it a relatively favourable carbohydrate source compared to white rice or white bread, though the glycemic load per full serving remains significant. The dish contains gluten and soy allergens and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or soy allergy. Garlic noodle is widely available in Singapore hawker-style cafés and is listed on field survey data under café menus such as The Light Cafe.
common_user_queries: How many calories in garlic noodle?
Garlic noodle vs aglio e olio

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for garlic noodle as a named dish was found. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB stir-fried noodle category data and cross-checked with USDA SR Legacy cooked noodle values, with adjustment for oil and seasoning. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried noodle category and USDA SR Legacy cooked noodle data; no exact garlic noodle entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source reference for this dish category. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as this is a plant-based noodle dish with no significant B12 source and the value is not reported in the adapted source. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 3. The input cuisine tag 'Italian' has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as the dish is a Singapore café preparation; the Italian cuisine tags in the input reflect the restaurant's broader menu (pizza, coffee, toast) rather than the dish's own culinary identity. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as refined vegetable cooking oils used in Singapore café cooking are not expected to contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA reference data. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry; no direct GI measurement for garlic noodle as a prepared dish was found in approved sources. 6. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce (mollusc-derived) is a standard ingredient; halal-certified versions may exist but cannot be assumed for this general record.
